content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 1, 2009

Frank L. Mecham
Supervisor, First District
1055 Monterey, Room D430
San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file No. I-09-026

Dear Mr. Mecham:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based solely on the facts presented to us in your request. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest.

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in decisions by the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors on litigation against the Board involving property located near property in which you have a 25 percent ownership interest?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-09-026
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts as you describe them, it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding the litigation would have a material financial effect on your property.
Therefore, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.

FACTS

In 2008, you inherited a 25 percent interest in property located in the town of
Santa Margarita, worth at least $2,000. As of January 6, 2009, you serve on the San Luis
Obispo County Board of Supervisors (the "Board").

Before you took your position on the Board, the Board approved Tentative Tract
Map 2586 and Conditional Use Permit for the Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC. Two
environmental non-profit groups filed suit against the County of San Luis Obispo
challenging the Board's approval of Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC's residential
development plan. This plan involves building a residential tract subdivision on a section
of the Santa Margarita Ranch, a 14,000 acre plot of land within San Luis Obispo County.
The area where this residential subdivision would be built is about a mile or more from
your property. In our telephone conversations, you explained that the residential
subdivision is part of a larger scheme of development for Santa Margarita Ranch, and
that if this portion of the development moves forward, other areas are slated for
development as well. If this subdivision is built out, there are plans to continue building
on the 14,000 acres.

During our telephone conversations, you stated that the pending law suit
challenges the Board's approval of a plan that would allow Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC
to build a residential subdivision in Santa Margarita, about a mile or more from your
property. It is your opinion that the outcome of this law suit will have a direct bearing on
the development of the rest of the phases planned for the Santa Margarita Ranch. You
also stated that the phases of development are interrelated and the outcome of the
residential subdivision could directly affect those areas of development that are within
500 feet of your property.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes
a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

File No. I-09-026
Page No. 3

Step One: Are you a Public Official?
As a member of the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors, you are a
public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, you may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use your official position to influence any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic
interests. (Regulations 18702.1-18702.4.)

Step Two: Will you be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use your official position to influence a governmental decision?
You have stated that you expect that decisions regarding a lawsuit challenging the
subdivision development will come before the Board. For purposes of this analysis, we
assume that you will have decisions before you related to the lawsuit; the analysis that
follows applies to those potential decisions.

Step Three: Do you have a financial interest in the decisions at issue?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic
interests include:

1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a
director or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a),
Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in any business entity in
which a public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(d), Regulation 18703.1(b).)

2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b),
Regulation 18703.2.)

3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the
public official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 18703.3.)

4. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)

5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of
his or her immediate family. This is also known as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.)

File No. I-09-026
Page No. 4

Based on the facts you provided, you have an interest in the property within the
town of Santa Margarita in which you have a 25 percent ownership interest. You have
stated that this interest is at least $2,000. You have not provided information regarding
any other economic interests that might bear on this analysis; we therefore only address
the Santa Margarita property.

Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of
the boundaries of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision.
(Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).) If the real property is not directly involved, it is indirectly
involved. (Regulation 18704(a).)

Your property is greater than 500 feet away from the property that is the subject
of the law suit about which the Board will be making decisions. The Act presumes that
the property is indirectly involved.

Step Five: Materiality Standard

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700.4.) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard in Regulation
18705.2(b) applies. That is, because your property is more than 500 feet from the
property that is the subject of the governmental decisions, the effects of the decisions are
presumed not to be material. The presumption can be rebutted with proof that the
circumstances suggest that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decisions
will have a material financial effect on the property. (Regulation 18705.2(b).) A partial
list of circumstances you would consider when determining materiality include:

(A) The development potential or income producing potential
of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;

(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of
use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.
(Regulation 18702.5(b)(A)-(C).)

The project that is the subject of the lawsuit about which the Board will be
making decisions is a residential subdivision about a mile or more from your property.
You have not provided facts that could rebut the presumption of materiality, but given the

File No. 1-09-026
Page No. 5

factors above, among others that might apply to your circumstances, you can determine
whether there are any factors affecting your property that would be material.³

**Step Six: Is it Reasonably Foreseeable that the Decision Will Have a
Material Financial Impact on your property interest?**

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (*In re Thorner* (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

During our telephone conversations, you stated that the pending law suit
challenges the Board's approval of a plan that would allow Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC
to build a residential subdivision in Santa Margarita, about a mile or more from your
property. It is your opinion that the outcome of this law suit will have a direct bearing on
the development of the rest of the phases planned for the Santa Margarita Ranch. You
also stated that the phases of development are interrelated and the outcome of the
residential subdivision could directly affect those areas of development that are within
500 feet of your property. Given this information, your opinion seems to be that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decisions the Board will be making
regarding the pending lawsuit will have a material financial effect on your property in
Santa Margarita.

**Step 7: Does the "Public Generally" Exception Apply?**

Even when there is a disqualifying conflict of interest, the "public generally"
exception sometimes applies to allow participation by a public official who is otherwise
unable to participate in governmental decisions.

"Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's
economic interests is material, a public official does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decision if
the official can establish that the governmental decision will affect
the public official's economic interests in a manner [that] is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect
the public generally as set forth in Title 2 California Code of
Regulations Sections 18707.1 - 18707.9." (Regulation 18707.)

___________________________

³ We have previously advised that the mere existence of additional factors is not sufficient to rebut
the presumption of non-materiality. The presumption that an indirectly involved property will not be
materially affected is rebutted only if the governmental decision will have a clearly distinguishable and
substantial financial effect on an official's property. (See *Matranga* Advice Letter, A-07-064; *Berger*
Advice Letter, A-05-054.)

File No. I-09-026
Page No. 6


If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a governmental decision
on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the
public generally," then the public official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103;
Regulations 18700(b)(7), 18707(a).)

To come under the purview of the public generally exception, the decision
affecting the public official's economic interests must also affect a significant segment of
the public generally in substantially the same manner as it affects the public official.
(Regulation 18707.1(b)(1) and (2).) The public generally exception applies if both
prongs of this two-prong test are met. First the official must determine if the decision
affects a significant segment of the population. If the prong is met, then the official must
determine that the segment is affected in substantially the same manner as the public
official.

For real property, the "significant segment" test is met if the decision in question
also affects ten percent or more of all property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency or the district the official represents, to qualify under the public generally
exception. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(B)(i).) This exception also applies if the decision
affects 5,000 property owners in the official's jurisdiction or district. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1)(B)(ii).)

Because we do not have facts to determine whether this exception would apply in
your situation, we provide this information for you to base your decision regarding
whether the public generally exception applies.⁴

Step Eight: Does the Legally Required Exception Apply?

The facts as you describe them do not suggest that this exception would apply; we
therefore do not analyze this step.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

Enclosure

⁴ Information provided on the San Luis Obispo website seems to indicate that the subject property
is not located in your district. This would make it highly unlikely, given the known facts, that the public
generally exception would apply.

decisions by the San Luis obispo County Board of Supervisors on litigation against the
Board involving property located near property in which you have a 25 percen"t
ownership interest?
I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in sections l8l l0 through 1g997 of ritle 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory retbrences are to Title 2, Division-6 of the California Code of
Regulaticlns. unless otherwise indicated.

formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; Regulation tSi:StctiJl. c.py enclosed-)

File No. l-09-026

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts as you describe them, it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding the litigation would have a material financial effect on your property.
Therefore, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. QUESTION Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in decisions by the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors on litigation against the Board involving property located near property in which you have a 25 percent ownership interest? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) Therefore, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest. FACTS In 2008, you inherited a 25 percent interest in property located in the town of Santa Margarita, worth at least $2,000. As of January 6, 2009, you serve on the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors (the "Board"). Before you took your position on the Board, the Board approved Tentative Tract Map 2586 and Conditional Use Permit for the Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC. Two environmental non-profit groups filed suit against the County of San Luis Obispo challenging the Board's approval of Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC's residential development plan. This plan involves building a residential tract subdivision on a section of the Santa Margarita Ranch, a 14,000 acre plot of land within San Luis Obispo County. The area where this residential subdivision would be built is about a mile or more from your property. In our telephone conversations, you explained that the residential subdivision is part of a larger scheme of development for Santa Margarita Ranch, and that if this portion of the
